topics.title: Spring Core — IoC & Dependency Injection
topics.description: Spring's IoC (Inversion of Control) container manages object creation and dependency wiring. Instead of your code creating dependencies (new MyService()), you declare dependencies and Spring injects them. DI (Dependency Injection) is the mechanism: constructor injection (preferred), setter injection, or field injection (@Autowired). The ApplicationContext is the central container that holds all Spring beans throughout their lifecycle.
topics.story: Without IoC: you build a house yourself — you must find every contractor (new Plumber(), new Electrician()). With IoC: you hire a general contractor (Spring container). You describe what rooms you need and who depends on whom. The contractor handles finding, creating, and wiring all workers. You just define the requirements — Spring does the construction. This is Inversion of Control: you give up control of object creation to the framework.
topics.analogy: ApplicationContext = Amazon warehouse. @Bean = item listed in the catalog. @Autowired = 'I need this item'. Spring = warehouse robot that fetches the item and delivers it to your door (injects it). Singleton scope = warehouse has exactly one copy; everyone gets the same instance. Prototype scope = warehouse prints a fresh copy for every request. @Primary = preferred supplier when multiple vendors offer the same item.
topics.memoryAnchor: @Component/@Service/@Repository/@Controller = stereotype annotations, triggers component scan. @Autowired = inject by type. @Qualifier('name') = inject by name when multiple candidates. @Bean = manual factory method in @Configuration class. Scopes: singleton (default), prototype, request, session. @PostConstruct = init after injection. @PreDestroy = cleanup before destroy. Constructor injection → immutable, testable. Field injection → concise but hides deps.
topics.firstPrinciples: IoC inverts the dependency creation flow. In traditional code: A creates B creates C. With IoC: C is created first, injected into B, then B is injected into A. The framework manages the dependency graph and lifecycle. This decouples classes from their collaborators — you depend on interfaces, not implementations.
topics.bruteForce: Manual wiring: UserRepository repo = new UserRepositoryImpl(); UserService svc = new UserServiceImpl(repo); UserController ctrl = new UserController(svc); Must repeat this in every entry point. Changing an implementation requires modifying every construction site. Testing requires manually passing mocks. No lifecycle management.
topics.optimizedApproach: @Service class UserServiceImpl { @Autowired UserRepository repo; } Spring scans, instantiates, wires. Testing: @MockBean UserRepository repo — Spring auto-injects the mock. Change implementation: just annotate new class with @Service @Primary. Zero change in dependent classes. Bean lifecycle: instantiate → inject → @PostConstruct → ready → @PreDestroy → destroy.
topics.whenToUse: Constructor injection: mandatory dependencies, immutability, testability (preferred). Setter injection: optional dependencies. Field injection: avoid — hides dependencies, makes testing harder. @Qualifier: when you have multiple implementations (UserRepository, AdminRepository both implement Repository). @Primary: default bean when no qualifier. @Profile: activate different beans in dev/prod.

examples.title: Constructor Injection, Lifecycle, and Bean Scopes
examples.explanation: Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit and the class testable without Spring — just new UserService(mockRepo, mockNotifier). @PostConstruct runs after all dependencies are injected. @PreDestroy runs before the context closes (good for releasing resources). Singleton scope means one UserService instance for the entire app — shared state must be thread-safe.
examples.realWorldUse: Every Spring Boot service class uses constructor injection. @Repository on JPA repositories — Spring adds transaction and exception translation. @Service for business logic. @Controller/@RestController for HTTP layer. @PostConstruct used for loading caches, starting background threads, validating config.

examples.title: Multiple Implementations — @Primary, @Qualifier, @Profile
examples.explanation: @Primary resolves ambiguity when multiple beans implement the same interface — the @Primary bean is chosen by default. @Qualifier('name') overrides and selects a specific bean by name. @Profile('test') activates the bean only when the 'test' Spring profile is active — great for swapping implementations between environments without code changes.
examples.realWorldUse: Multiple datasource configurations (primary vs secondary DB). Notification service: EmailService (primary), SmsService (@Qualifier). Feature flags via @Profile. Testing: @MockBean in @SpringBootTest replaces real beans. Cache providers: caffeine (default) vs redis (@Qualifier).

problems.title: Constructor Injection Without Spring
problems.description: Without using any framework, demonstrate the IoC principle manually: create interfaces Logger and Database, implementations ConsoleLogger and H2Database, and a UserService that depends on both. Wire everything in main() using constructor injection. Then demonstrate how easy it is to swap ConsoleLogger with FileLogger.
problems.pattern: Manual IoC, constructor injection, interface-based design
problems.hint1: Define Logger interface with log(String) and Database with findUser(int).
problems.hint2: UserService constructor takes (Database, Logger) — no new() inside UserService.
problems.hint3: In main: Logger log = new ConsoleLogger(); Database db = new H2Database(); UserService svc = new UserService(db, log);

problems.title: Bean Scope Singleton vs Prototype Simulation
problems.description: Simulate Spring's Singleton and Prototype scope without the framework. Create a SimpleContainer class with registerSingleton(name, supplier) and registerPrototype(name, supplier). getBean(name) should return the same instance for singletons and a new instance for prototypes.
problems.pattern: Singleton pattern, factory, IoC container design
problems.hint1: Singleton: lazy-initialize and cache in a HashMap.
problems.hint2: Prototype: call the supplier every time getBean() is called.
problems.hint3: Use Map<String, Object> for singleton cache and Map<String, Supplier<Object>> for both.

problems.title: Detect Circular Dependency
problems.description: Two beans A and B have circular constructor injection: A depends on B, B depends on A. This causes a circular dependency error. Demonstrate the problem and show the fix using setter injection to break the cycle.
problems.pattern: Circular dependency, constructor vs setter injection
problems.hint1: With constructor injection, A can't be created without B, and B can't be created without A → deadlock.
problems.hint2: Fix: inject one side via setter after construction.
problems.hint3: In Spring, constructor circular injection fails at startup; setter injection works but should signal redesign need.
